summary_sections.title: Lesson Overview
summary_sections.content_html:
Overview

This lesson covers the fundamental measures of center used to analyze and summarize data sets. You'll learn how to calculate and apply the mean, median, and mode, and understand when each is most appropriate.

summary_sections.title: Definitions and Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Definitions

What is spermatogenesis?

Spermatogenesis is the complete process of sperm cell development, beginning with diploid spermatogonia that undergo mitosis and then meiosis within the seminiferous tubules to produce haploid spermatids, which are subsequently remodeled into mature spermatozoa through spermiogenesis.

What is the blood-testis barrier?

The blood-testis barrier is formed by tight junctions between adjacent Sertoli cells, creating an immunologically privileged environment that prevents immune cells from contacting and attacking developing sperm, which express unique surface antigens not recognized as 'self' by the immune system.

What is a vasectomy?

A vasectomy is a male sterilization procedure in which the vas deferens on each side is surgically cut, tied, or sealed to prevent sperm from entering the ejaculate. Testosterone production and ejaculation still occur, but the semen contains no sperm.

What is benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH)?

Benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH) is a non-cancerous enlargement of the prostate gland that commonly occurs in men over age 50. As the prostate grows, it compresses the prostatic urethra, causing symptoms like urinary hesitancy, weak stream, frequency, and nocturia.

What is inhibin?

Inhibin is a glycoprotein hormone produced by Sertoli cells in males (and granulosa cells in females) that provides selective negative feedback to the anterior pituitary gland, specifically inhibiting FSH secretion to help regulate the rate of spermatogenesis.

What is the ejaculatory duct?

The ejaculatory duct is a short paired duct formed by the junction of the vas deferens and the duct of the seminal vesicle. It passes through the prostate gland and opens into the prostatic urethra, delivering both sperm and seminal vesicle secretions during ejaculation.

A 68-year-old man visits his doctor complaining of difficulty starting urination, a weak stream, and waking up multiple times at night to urinate. A digital rectal exam reveals a symmetrically enlarged, smooth, firm prostate. What is the most likely diagnosis?

The combination of obstructive urinary symptoms (hesitancy, weak stream, nocturia) with a symmetrically enlarged, smooth prostate on digital rectal exam is classic for BPH. In contrast, prostate cancer typically presents as an irregular, hard nodule, though the two conditions can coexist.

A 16-year-old male athlete presents to the ER with sudden onset of severe left testicular pain and swelling that started during a soccer game. The affected testis appears higher in the scrotum and is extremely tender. What is the most likely diagnosis, and why is urgent treatment critical?

Testicular torsion is a surgical emergency in which the spermatic cord twists, cutting off the arterial blood supply to the testis. The classic presentation is sudden, severe testicular pain with a high-riding testis in a young male. Surgical detorsion must be performed within approximately 6 hours to prevent irreversible ischemic damage and testicular loss.

A newborn male is examined and the pediatrician notes that the right testis cannot be palpated in the scrotum. Ultrasound confirms it is located in the inguinal canal. The parents are counseled about the need for treatment. What is the primary concern if this condition is left untreated?

Cryptorchidism (undescended testis) left untreated increases the risk of testicular cancer by 5-10 times and causes impaired spermatogenesis because the inguinal canal temperature is too high for normal sperm development. Orchiopexy (surgical relocation of the testis to the scrotum) is typically performed by age 1 to reduce these risks.

A man who underwent a vasectomy two years ago asks his doctor whether the procedure has affected his testosterone levels or sexual function. What is the most accurate response?

A vasectomy only cuts the vas deferens to prevent sperm from reaching the ejaculate. Leydig cells continue producing testosterone normally (since their blood supply is unaffected), and ejaculation still occurs because seminal vesicle and prostate secretions make up the majority of semen volume. Only the sperm component is absent.

A medical student is studying erectile physiology and learns that erection involves vascular changes in the penis. A patient with long-standing diabetes and peripheral vascular disease reports progressive erectile dysfunction. What is the most likely underlying mechanism?

Erectile dysfunction in diabetic patients is commonly caused by vascular disease (damage to the endothelium of penile arteries) and diabetic neuropathy (damage to the parasympathetic nerves that mediate erection). Diabetes accelerates atherosclerosis and impairs nitric oxide-mediated vasodilation, reducing blood flow to the corpora cavernosa needed for erection.

summary_sections.title: Outliers and Resistant Measures
summary_sections.content_html:
Outliers and Resistant Measures

Testicular torsion is a surgical emergency in which the spermatic cord twists, cutting off the arterial blood supply to the testis. The classic presentation is sudden, severe testicular pain with a high-riding testis in a young male. Surgical detorsion must be performed within approximately 6 hours to prevent irreversible ischemic damage and testicular loss.

A vasectomy only cuts the vas deferens to prevent sperm from reaching the ejaculate. Leydig cells continue producing testosterone normally (since their blood supply is unaffected), and ejaculation still occurs because seminal vesicle and prostate secretions make up the majority of semen volume. Only the sperm component is absent.

summary_sections.title: Real-World Applications
summary_sections.content_html:
Real-World Applications

Example: The seminal vesicles contribute approximately what percentage of semen volume?

The seminal vesicles produce approximately 60-70% of the total semen volume, secreting a viscous, alkaline fluid rich in fructose (which provides energy for sperm), prostaglandins, and clotting proteins that support sperm viability and function.

Example: A 68-year-old man visits his doctor complaining of difficulty starting urination, a weak stream, and waking up multiple times at night to urinate. A digital rectal exam reveals a symmetrically enlarged, smooth, firm prostate. What is the most likely diagnosis?

The combination of obstructive urinary symptoms (hesitancy, weak stream, nocturia) with a symmetrically enlarged, smooth prostate on digital rectal exam is classic for BPH. In contrast, prostate cancer typically presents as an irregular, hard nodule, though the two conditions can coexist.

Example: A newborn male is examined and the pediatrician notes that the right testis cannot be palpated in the scrotum. Ultrasound confirms it is located in the inguinal canal. The parents are counseled about the need for treatment. What is the primary concern if this condition is left untreated?

Cryptorchidism (undescended testis) left untreated increases the risk of testicular cancer by 5-10 times and causes impaired spermatogenesis because the inguinal canal temperature is too high for normal sperm development. Orchiopexy (surgical relocation of the testis to the scrotum) is typically performed by age 1 to reduce these risks.
